Hello, This Is My Lab 
This site is a living documentation of how I think, build, and break things. Not a portfolio — a lab notebook. I've been meaning to build this for a long time. lab notebook Who this is for Primarily for me. If you find value here, that's a bonus. I write to think clearly. When I explain something — a system, a concept, a decision — I understand it better. This site is the discipline of doing that in public. What you'll find here Articles Perspectives Projects Docs Resources On writing style I prefer direct sentences. I'll tell you what I think, not what "it could be argued." If I'm uncertain, I'll say so. I also believe that restraint is a design choice.

so. I also believe that restraint is a design choice. This site won't have comment sections, likes, or a following count. It's not optimized for engagement — it's optimized for thinking. On the design feel Welcome to the lab. ve been meaning to build this for a long time."}),"

AI Won't Replace Engineers — It Will Expose Bad Ones 
The fear isn't about automation. It's about accountability. AI doesn't replace the engineer — it removes the excuse. AI is going to replace software engineers. I think this gets the causality backwards. The real pressure isn't replacement define the problem correctly The engineer's job has always been, at its core, a translation job: take an ambiguous human need, convert it into a precise specification, and implement it in a system that will fail in ways no one anticipated. AI doesn't do that. It does the third part reasonably well, when the first two parts have already been done.

well, when the first two parts have already been done. What AI actually exposes Consider an engineer who relied on their ability to write CRUD endpoints quickly. That skill is now table stakes — anyone with a good prompt can do it. The differentiator has shifted. producing code volume This is not a threat. It's a clarification. The floor is rising, not the ceiling The engineering ceiling — the complexity of problems that require genuine systems thinking, security intuition, domain expertise, and architectural judgment — hasn't changed. What's changed is the floor.

architectural judgment — hasn't changed. What's changed is the floor. works should exist Where I might be wrong If the floor rises faster than junior engineers can build fundamentals, the path from beginner to competent engineer may become steeper, not easier. That's a real concern — not about AI replacing engineers, but about AI compressing the time available to develop judgment. The tool is neutral. The impact depends on what we do with the time it buys us. t replacement"}),"\n",t(r.p,{children:["AI tools — Copilot, Claude, Cursor — are extraordinarily good at generating boilerplate, suggesting implementations, and reducing the friction of writing obvious code.

NOVELMAN — A Web Novel Platform with AI at the Core 
Building a platform where AI doesn't replace the author — it amplifies the story. Architecture decisions, trade-offs, and lessons from building NOVELMAN. what if the reading experience was as considered as the writing experience? Most novel platforms are read-centric databases. The author uploads a file, readers page through chapters, ads run. NOVELMAN is designed differently: the AI is a collaborator in the authoring process, not an afterthought in the reader experience.

the authoring process, not an afterthought in the reader experience. The problem being solved Web novel authors — particularly indie authors — have three pain points: Consistency across long-form work Discovery Reader retention NOVELMAN targets all three, with AI as an enabling layer for the first and a better product design for the other two. Architecture The system is a Turborepo monorepo with three main apps: apps/web apps/studio apps/api The AI layer uses a multi-agent orchestration pattern: separate agents for continuity checking, character consistency, and style analysis, coordinated by a central router. Database design What I learned Turborepo has sharp edges.

router. Database design What I learned Turborepo has sharp edges. AI in authoring tools needs strict scoping. The reading experience is the product. Current status [ACTIVE] The most interesting decision was the content schema. Chapters are stored as structured prose (not raw HTML), with metadata layers: scene tags, POV character, location, time marker. This structure is what enables the AI to reason about continuity — you can't ask "is this consistent with chapter 12" if chapter 12 is just a blob of text.

Drizzle ORM with Supabase — A Practical Setup 
Type-safe database access with Drizzle ORM connected to Supabase PostgreSQL. Schema, migrations, and the patterns that actually work. 
Drizzle ORM gives you full TypeScript control over your SQL without the magic of an ORM that hides what it's doing. Combined with Supabase's managed PostgreSQL, it's a strong stack for production apps. Installation npm install drizzle-orm postgres 
npm install -D drizzle-kit

apps. Installation npm install drizzle-orm postgres npm install -D drizzle-kit 
Configure the connection Define your schema Drizzle schemas are just TypeScript. There's no magic, no decorators — you write the table definition and Drizzle infers the types: Generate and run migrations # Generate SQL migration from schema changes 
npx drizzle-kit generate 
# Push directly to the DB (good for early dev) 
npx drizzle-kit push

Agentic Intent classification using small, local LLMs instead of massive cloud APIs.
A "dark mode by default" design system that uses grain and subtle gradients instead of flat colors.
Using PostgreSQL triggers as a makeshift event bus for lightweight microservices.
The concept of "code rot" applies to ideas too. If you don't document a thought within 48 hours, the context is lost forever.

**Reflecting on architecture and simplicity.** 
Over the past few weeks, I've been diving deep into multi-agent systems and noticing a recurring pattern: we tend to build systems that are significantly more complex than the problem requires. 
When I built Ryuu v1, I used five different services. In v2, I reduced it to two. The system got faster, more reliable, and surprisingly, more intelligent. The constraint forced the agents to communicate directly instead of relying on a bloated orchestrator. 
The takeaway? Complexity is easy. Simplicity is an engineering achievement.
